category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Similar Questions/Part B/Calculus of Variations — Euler-Lagrange Equation

questiontext.text: The functional \(J[y]=\displaystyle\int_a^b F(x,y,y')\,dx\) has stationary functions satisfying:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\dfrac{\partial F}{\partial y}-\dfrac{d}{dx}\dfrac{\partial F}{\partial y'}=0\) (Euler-Lagrange equation)

The E-L equation is derived by computing the first variation \(\delta J=0\) and applying integration by parts. It is a necessary condition for a smooth extremal. The equation may be ODE or PDE depending on the number of independent variables. For \(F=F(x,y')\) (no explicit \(y\)): \(F_{y'}=\text{const}\) (Beltrami identity).

questiontext.text: The shortest path between two points in the plane is found by minimising \(J[y]=\displaystyle\int_{x_1}^{x_2}\sqrt{1+y'^2}\,dx\). The Euler-Lagrange equation gives:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(y''=0\) (straight lines)

\(F=\sqrt{1+y'^2}\). \(\partial F/\partial y=0\). E-L: \(-d/dx[y'/\sqrt{1+y'^2}]=0\Rightarrow y'/\sqrt{1+y'^2}=c\Rightarrow y'=\text{const}\Rightarrow y=ax+b\). Straight lines are geodesics in the Euclidean plane, confirming that the shortest path is a straight line (obvious, but the variational derivation is elegant).

questiontext.text: The brachistochrone problem (fastest descent under gravity) leads to a solution which is a:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) Cycloid

Brachistochrone (Johann Bernoulli, 1696): minimise time \(T=\int\sqrt{(1+y'^2)/(2gy)}dx\). The E-L equation yields the cycloid: \(x=a(\theta-\sin\theta)\), \(y=a(1-\cos\theta)\). The cycloid is the curve traced by a point on a rolling circle. It also has the tautochrone property (equal time from any starting point).

questiontext.text: The shape of a hanging chain (catenary) minimises the potential energy. It satisfies the equation:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(y=c_1\cosh\left(\frac{x-c_2}{c_1}\right)\)

Catenary: minimise \(\int y\sqrt{1+y'^2}dx\). E-L: \(\sqrt{1+y'^2}-y\cdot d/dx[y'/\sqrt{1+y'^2}]=0\). Solving: \(y=c_1\cosh((x-c_2)/c_1)\). The catenary appears in suspension bridges, cables, and is related to minimal surface (catenoid of revolution). \(\cosh\) is the hyperbolic cosine.

questiontext.text: If \(F\) does not explicitly depend on \(x\) (i.e., \(\partial F/\partial x=0\)), the E-L equation has a first integral:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(F-y'F_{y'}=\text{const}\) (Beltrami identity)

When \(F=F(y,y')\) (no explicit \(x\)): differentiating \(F-y'F_{y'}\) along an extremal using E-L gives \(d/dx(F-y'F_{y'})=0\). So \(F-y'F_{y'}=c\) (Beltrami identity / first integral). This reduces the E-L equation (2nd order ODE) to a 1st order ODE, simplifying solution. Used in brachistochrone, geodesic, catenary problems.

questiontext.text: The isoperimetric problem (maximum area enclosed by a curve of fixed length \(L\)) has solution:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) A circle of circumference \(L\)

Queen Dido's problem / isoperimetric inequality: among all closed curves of length \(L\), the circle encloses the maximum area \(A=L^2/(4\pi)\). Variational proof: using Lagrange multiplier (isoperimetric constraint), the E-L equation yields a circle. The isoperimetric inequality: \(4\pi A\leq L^2\) with equality for the circle.

questiontext.text: A necessary condition for \(y^*\) to be a local minimum (not just a stationary point) of \(J\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) The second variation \(\delta^2J\geq0\) for all admissible variations

Legendre condition: necessary for a minimum is \(F_{y'y'}\geq0\) along the extremal (non-negative second variation). Sufficient conditions include the Jacobi condition (no conjugate points) and Weierstrass excess function. The second variation analysis is analogous to the second derivative test in ordinary calculus.

questiontext.text: The surface of revolution \(2\pi\int y\sqrt{1+y'^2}\,dx\) with fixed endpoints has minimal area when \(y\) is a:
The surface of revolution \(2\pi\int_a^b y\sqrt{1+y'^2}\,dx\) with fixed endpoints is minimized when \(y\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) Catenary \(y=c_1\cosh((x-c_2)/c_1)\)

The Euler-Lagrange equation for \(\int y\sqrt{1+y'^2}dx\) gives the catenary (using Beltrami identity: \(y\sqrt{1+y'^2}-y\cdot y'^2/\sqrt{1+y'^2}=c\Rightarrow y/\sqrt{1+y'^2}=c_1\)). The minimal surface of revolution is a catenoid. If the endpoints are too far apart, the soap film breaks (no minimizer exists — Goldschmidt discontinuous solution).

questiontext.text: Minimise \(J[x]=\displaystyle\int_0^1(\dot{x}^2+x^2)\,dt\) with \(x(0)=0\), \(x(1)=1\). The Euler-Lagrange equation is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(\ddot{x}-x=0\)

\(F=\dot{x}^2+x^2\). \(F_x=2x\), \(F_{\dot x}=2\dot x\). E-L: \(2x-2\ddot x=0\Rightarrow\ddot x=x\). General solution: \(x=Ae^t+Be^{-t}\). With \(x(0)=0\): \(A+B=0\Rightarrow B=-A\). With \(x(1)=1\): \(A(e-e^{-1})=1\Rightarrow A=1/(e-e^{-1})\). So \(x^*(t)=\sinh(t)/\sinh(1)\).

name.text: CoV - x*(1/2) for BVP x(0)=0, x(1)=1

questiontext.text: For the extremal of \(J[x]=\int_0^1(\dot{x}^2+x^2)dt\) with \(x(0)=0\), \(x(1)=1\), the value \(x^*(1/2)\) equals:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(\dfrac{\sinh(1/2)}{\sinh(1)}=\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{e}-1/\sqrt{e}}\cdot\frac{\sqrt{e}-1/\sqrt{e}}{2}\)

From the previous: \(x^*(t)=\sinh(t)/\sinh(1)\). At \(t=1/2\): \(x^*(1/2)=\sinh(1/2)/\sinh(1)\). Now \(\sinh(1/2)=(e^{1/2}-e^{-1/2})/2=(\sqrt{e}-1/\sqrt{e})/2\) and \(\sinh(1)=(e-e^{-1})/2=(e-1/e)/2\). So \(x^*(1/2)=\frac{\sqrt{e}-1/\sqrt{e}}{e-1/e}=\frac{\sqrt{e}-1/\sqrt{e}}{e-1/e}\). Numerically \(\approx0.4577\). Closest option: \(\sinh(1/2)/\sinh(1)\).

questiontext.text: If the functional \(J[y]=\int_a^bF(x,y,y')dx\) is to be extremised with \(y(a)=y_0\) fixed but \(y(b)\) free (free boundary), the natural boundary condition at \(b\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(F_{y'}(b,y(b),y'(b))=0\)

Free-end condition: when the right endpoint is free, the transversality condition becomes \(F_{y'}|_{x=b}=0\). This is a natural boundary condition (vs essential boundary conditions which prescribe the value of \(y\)). It arises from the boundary term in integration by parts when deriving the E-L equation with free endpoints.

questiontext.text: To minimise \(J[y]=\int F\,dx\) subject to the constraint \(G[y]=\int G(x,y,y')\,dx=\ell\) (isoperimetric constraint), we form:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) The augmented functional \(J^*[y]=\int(F+\lambda G)\,dx\) and apply E-L to it

Isoperimetric problem: introduce Lagrange multiplier \(\lambda\) and extremise \(J^*=\int(F+\lambda G)dx\). The E-L equation for \(J^*\) gives: \((F+\lambda G)_y-d/dx(F+\lambda G)_{y'}=0\). The multiplier \(\lambda\) is determined by the constraint \(G[y]=\ell\). This is analogous to Lagrange multipliers in multivariable calculus.

answer.text: Apply E-L directly to \(J[y]\) and ignore the constraint

questiontext.text: For a functional \(J[y]=\int F(x,y,y',y'')\,dx\), the Euler-Lagrange equation is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(F_y-\frac{d}{dx}F_{y'}+\frac{d^2}{dx^2}F_{y''}=0\)

For functionals depending on higher derivatives, each derivative adds a term with alternating sign: \(F_y-\frac{d}{dx}F_{y'}+\frac{d^2}{dx^2}F_{y''}-\cdots=0\). For the beam equation (elastic energy \(\sim\int y''^2dx\)): \(F=y''^2\), so \(F_y=0,F_{y'}=0,F_{y''}=2y''\). E-L: \(2\frac{d^2}{dx^2}y''=0\Rightarrow y^{(4)}=0\).

questiontext.text: Hamilton's principle in mechanics states that the motion of a system between times \(t_1\) and \(t_2\) extremises the action:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(S=\int_{t_1}^{t_2}L(q,\dot{q},t)\,dt\) where \(L=T-V\) is the Lagrangian

Hamilton's principle (principle of least action): the physical trajectory extremises \(S=\int L\,dt\) where \(L=T-V\) (kinetic minus potential energy). The E-L equations for this functional give the Euler-Lagrange (Newton's) equations of motion: \(\frac{d}{dt}\frac{\partial L}{\partial\dot q_i}-\frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i}=0\).

questiontext.text: In the variable endpoint (transversality) problem, if the endpoint lies on a curve \(x=\phi(y)\), the transversality condition is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \([F+(y'-\phi^{-1}(y_b)')F_{y'}]_{x=b}=0\)

Transversality condition: when the terminal point lies on a prescribed curve, the extremal must meet the curve at an angle satisfying a specific orthogonality condition (analogous to perpendicularity in geometry). The exact form depends on the functional. For the arc-length functional, the transversality condition means the extremal is perpendicular to the terminal curve.

answer.text: A transversality condition relating \(F\) and \(F_{y'}\) at the endpoint
No condition is needed at the free endpoint

questiontext.text: A weak local minimum of \(J[y]\) is an extremum where \(J[y^*]\leq J[y^*+\eta]\) for all \(\eta\) with:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (A) \(\|\eta\|_{C^1}\) small (both \(|\eta|\) and \(|\eta'|\) small)

Weak extremum: \(J[y^*]\leq J[y]\) for all \(y\) close to \(y^*\) in the \(C^1\) norm (small \(|\eta|\) and small \(|\eta'|\)). Strong extremum: close in \(C^0\) norm only (small \(|\eta|\) but \(|\eta'|\) can be large). A strong minimum is also a weak minimum, but not vice versa. The Weierstrass condition is needed for strong minima.

questiontext.text: The Pontryagin Maximum Principle is a generalization of the calculus of variations to:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) Optimal control problems where controls may be discontinuous or constrained

Pontryagin (1956): the maximum principle handles optimal control problems with bounded/discontinuous controls, state constraints, and terminal conditions. For unconstrained smooth problems, it reduces to the E-L equations. It introduces co-state variables (adjoint system) and the Hamiltonian \(H(x,u,\psi)=f(x,u)+\psi g(x,u)\). The optimal control maximises \(H\).

question.single: truetrueabc Computing the minimum surface area of a soap film Optimal control with possibly discontinuous or constrained controls Solving only linear optimization problems The classical E-L equations without any extension

questiontext.text: The Jacobi condition for a local minimum of \(J[y]\) requires that the extremal \([a,b]\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) Contains no conjugate point of \(a\) in the interior \((a,b]\)

Jacobi condition: necessary for a weak local minimum is that \((a,b]\) contains no conjugate point of \(a\). A conjugate point is where a solution of the Jacobi equation (the linearised E-L equation) vanishes. If a conjugate point exists inside the interval, the extremal is not a local minimum (it can be made shorter/smaller).

answer.text: Has the same E-L equation as the original functional
Contains no conjugate point of \(a\) in the open interval \((a,b)\)

questiontext.text: For \(J[y]=\int_0^T\!(ay'^2+by^2)\,dt\) with \(y(0)=y_0\) and \(y(T)=y_T\), the Euler-Lagrange equation is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (B) \(ay''-by=0\) (linear ODE)

\(F=ay'^2+by^2\). \(F_y=2by\), \(F_{y'}=2ay'\). E-L: \(2by-2ay''=0\Rightarrow ay''-by=0\). If \(b>0\) and \(a>0\): \(y''=\frac{b}{a}y\), hyperbolic type. If \(b<0\): \(y''=-|b/a|y\), trigonometric solutions. The nature of the extremal depends on the sign of \(b/a\).

questiontext.text: The extremal of \(J[y]=\displaystyle\int_0^1(y'^2-y^2)\,dt\), \(y(0)=0\), \(y(1)=1\) satisfies the E-L equation \(y''+y=0\). The extremal is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (C) \(y=\sin(t)/\sin(1)\)

E-L for \(F=y'^2-y^2\): \(-2y-2y''=0\Rightarrow y''+y=0\). General solution: \(y=A\cos t+B\sin t\). \(y(0)=0\Rightarrow A=0\). \(y(1)=B\sin(1)=1\Rightarrow B=1/\sin(1)\). Extremal: \(y=\sin(t)/\sin(1)\).
